reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs> with TypeScript — no YAML blocks, no StacktapeConfig type, no fenced TypeScript blocks for config.
Concrete 'When to use' / 'When NOT to use' H2 sections provide real decision guidance (fan-out → SNS, streaming → Kinesis, request-response → invoke directly).
Standard vs FIFO comparison table is well-organized, source-grounded on throughput numbers, ordering, and delivery semantics.
Dead-letter queue section leads with the 'poison message' problem that motivates the feature, then covers configuration — exactly the decision-support framing required.
Lambda trigger section includes both config (SqsIntegration class with batchSize/maxBatchWindowSeconds) and a working handler snippet — practical end-to-end coverage.
ConnectTo section names the exact injected env vars (STP_TASK_QUEUE_ARN / _NAME / _URL) anchored to the example resource name 'taskQueue', with a matching SDK usage snippet.

FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (dead letters, container consumers), AWS service questions (pricing, max message size, retention), and comparison/choosing (SQS vs SNS, SQS vs Kinesis).
Long polling recommendation ('Set to 20 for most workloads') is explicitly sourced from the JSDoc and is usefully opinionated.
Alarm section covers both trigger types (sqs-queue-not-empty and sqs-queue-received-messages-count) with source-grounded descriptions.
Single <ApiReference definitionName='SqsQueueProps' /> at the bottom — correct definitionName, correct count.
problems: The 'Connecting to other resources' section lists eight specific IAM action names (sqs:SendMessage, sqs:ReceiveMessage, sqs:DeleteMessage, sqs:GetQueueAttributes, sqs:GetQueueUrl, sqs:ListQueueTags, sqs:PurgeQueue, sqs:ChangeMessageVisibility) that do not appear in any provided source file. The source only states the generic 'send/receive/delete' summary. Actions like sqs:ListQueueTags and sqs:PurgeQueue may be accurate but cannot be verified from the provided sources — stating them as fact is an ungrounded claim that could mislead readers.

The <Tip> callout 'Match the queue type of your DLQ to the source queue. If the main queue is FIFO, the DLQ must also be FIFO.' is not stated or implied in the provided source files. It is a true AWS constraint, but presenting it as Stacktape-specific guidance without a source reference is a source-grounding gap.
optionalImprovements: Replace the specific IAM action list in 'Connecting to other resources' with the source-grounded summary ('send, receive, and delete messages') plus a note that full permissions are managed by Stacktape internally. This avoids stating actions that aren't in the source and removes maintenance risk if the permission set ever changes.

Attribute the FIFO DLQ tip as an AWS SQS constraint rather than leaving it as unattributed guidance — e.g., 'Amazon SQS requires the DLQ to be the same type as the source queue: if the source is FIFO, the DLQ must also be FIFO.' This makes the source of the constraint clear to the reader.

The 'Basic example' section says Stacktape creates 'a standard queue with sensible defaults: 256 KB max message size, 4-day retention, 30-second visibility timeout, and short polling' — all are correct and sourced. Consider also noting that no properties are required at all (the empty object `{}` is valid), which reinforces the zero-friction message for first-time readers.

The FAQ answer for 'How much does Amazon SQS cost?' gives specific per-request prices ($0.40/$0.50 per million). These are correct list prices as of writing but SQS pricing does change; consider framing as approximate ('roughly $0.40–$0.50 per million requests depending on queue type') to reduce staleness risk.

The EventBridge integration example uses a plain-object literal for the events array entry (no `new` constructor), while the Lambda trigger section uses `new SqsIntegration(...)`. Adding a one-sentence comment in the EventBridge example noting that this integration is configured as a plain object (since no exported class exists for this integration type) would prevent reader confusion about the inconsistent style.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Standard vs FIFO comparison table is excellent — throughput numbers, ordering guarantees, delivery semantics, and use cases all in one scannable grid.
Code examples are realistic and complete, including an actual Lambda handler implementation and AWS SDK send-message snippet with correct env var names.
Decision support is consistent throughout each section: when to enable, when the default is fine, cost/tradeoff signal.
connectTo environment variable names correctly follow the STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM] pattern using the example resource name 'taskQueue'.
FAQ has exactly 10 Q&A pairs spanning Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, and comparison questions as required.
No forbidden patterns detected: all Stacktape config is in <CodeBlock intellisense>, no YAML resources: blocks, no StacktapeConfig type import, no fenced TypeScript with defineConfig.
When NOT to use is a peer H2 (not nested under When to use), and names concrete alternatives for each anti-use-case.
Alarm section correctly identifies both SQS alarm trigger types from the source (sqs-queue-not-empty and sqs-queue-received-messages-count).
problems: The 'Connecting to other resources' section lists eight specific IAM actions (sqs:GetQueueAttributes, sqs:GetQueueUrl, sqs:ListQueueTags, sqs:PurgeQueue, sqs:ChangeMessageVisibility, plus the three send/receive/delete) as granted by connectTo. The source files only document 'send/receive/delete' in plain language — the five additional actions are unsupported Stacktape-specific claims. A production engineer relying on this list to scope IAM trust or audit permissions could be misled if the list is incomplete or inaccurate.

The policyStatements property on SqsQueueProps (for cross-account access, allowing SNS to send to the queue, etc.) has no mention anywhere in the prose. This is a meaningful operational feature — SNS-to-SQS fan-out is a canonical pattern that requires a resource policy statement. Omitting it entirely leaves a gap for teams integrating with SNS or external AWS accounts.

The Alarms section code example only shows sqs-queue-not-empty. The sqs-queue-received-messages-count trigger is mentioned in prose but not demonstrated — it requires a thresholdCount value, which is non-obvious and worth showing.

FAQ pricing answer gives specific per-message dollar figures ($0.40/$0.50 per million) without a pricing source file in the provided sources. The FAQ rules allow well-known AWS facts, but specific dollar prices drift quickly and the style guide says to avoid inventing prices not in the source.
mandatoryFixes: Replace the enumerated IAM actions list in 'Connecting to other resources' with source-grounded language: the source documents 'send, receive, and delete' access. Either remove the specific action list and say 'Stacktape grants send, receive, and delete permissions along with the ancillary actions needed to use the queue URL', or add a caveat that the exact action set is managed by Stacktape and subject to change. Do not present unsourced Stacktape-specific IAM grants as a definitive list a production engineer can rely on.
optionalImprovements: Add a brief mention of policyStatements in a short 'Access control' or 'Cross-account access' subsection, or at minimum a sentence near the Alarms section: 'To allow an SNS topic or external AWS account to publish to this queue, add a policyStatements entry — see the API reference for the Effect/Action/Principal shape.'
Add a second alarm code example showing sqs-queue-received-messages-count with a concrete thresholdCount value so readers can copy it without hunting through the API reference.

Soften the FAQ pricing answer by removing the specific dollar figures and replacing with pricing model language: 'SQS is priced per request; the first 1 million requests per month are free, after which standard queues cost a fraction of a cent per thousand requests — check the AWS pricing page for current rates by region.'

The 'Triggering a Lambda function' section says 'The function processes the batch and deletes successfully handled messages' — technically, with SQS/Lambda integration, Lambda deletes messages automatically on success; the function does not call DeleteMessage. Clarify this: 'AWS deletes successfully processed messages automatically; only messages that cause an exception remain visible for retry.'

Consider linking the first mention of 'dead-letter queue' to the AWS SQS concept page or the Stacktape alarms doc, since DLQ is a term some readers may not know without the brief definition already provided — the current inline explanation is fine but a link would help.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

issues.statement: the `sqs-queue-not-empty` alarm description says it fires when 'any of: visible messages, in-flight messages, messages received, or messages sent are non-zero'
issues.reason: This is accurately copied from the source JSDoc, but the prose in the Alarms section says 'fires when the queue has unprocessed messages' followed by the parenthetical. The word 'unprocessed' is slightly misleading — the alarm also fires when messages are 'sent' (newly added) or 'in-flight' (currently being processed), not just when they are 'unprocessed'. The parenthetical corrects it, but the leading phrase could confuse a reader skimming.
issues.suggestedFix: Change 'fires when the queue has unprocessed messages' to 'fires when the queue has activity' or 'fires when the queue is not idle', then keep the parenthetical listing the four metrics.

evidence.quote: The queue is considered "not empty" if any of these are non-zero: visible messages, in-flight messages, messages received, or messages sent.

The draft correctly names both in prose but only shows a code example for `sqs-queue-not-empty`. The `sqs-queue-received-messages-count` trigger requires a `thresholdCount` property, which isn't demonstrated. verifierResults.summary: The draft is exceptionally well-grounded. Every Stacktape-specific claim — defaults, property names, IAM permissions, alarm triggers, referenceable parameters, redrivePolicy constraints — was verified against source files and found accurate. All cross-links are valid. The heading hierarchy is correct. Code examples use valid class names with correct imports. The FAQ covers all three required categories with 10 questions. Only two low-severity issues found: a slightly misleading leading phrase in the alarm description ('unprocessed messages' when the metric also covers in-flight and sent messages), and the absence of a code example for the sqs-queue-received-messages-count alarm trigger despite it having a required `thresholdCount` property. No high or medium severity issues.

The draft is mostly grounded in the provided SQS queue types and trigger definitions. The main problems are a few Stacktape-specific claims that go beyond the source, plus one likely-invalid config example for class-based trigger usage.

issues.statement: The Lambda SQS trigger example imports and instantiates `SqsIntegration` with `new SqsIntegration({ ... })`.
issues.reason: The provided source defines `SqsIntegration` as an interface with `type: 'sqs'` and nested `properties`, not as a class constructor shape. The draft’s class-style example is not supported by the provided type definition and is likely to fail the code-block validator unless the class exists in a source file not provided here.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the source-grounded object shape for the trigger, for example `events: [{ type: 'sqs', properties: { sqsQueueName: 'taskQueue', batchSize: 10, maxBatchWindowSeconds: 5 } }]`, unless a provided runtime export proves a `SqsIntegration` class constructor exists.

issues.statement: “Configure `redrivePolicy` with either `targetSqsQueueName` (another queue in the same stack) or `targetSqsQueueArn` (an external queue). You must specify exactly one.”
issues.reason: The source documents both optional target properties but does not state that exactly one must be specified. This may be true in runtime validation, but it is not grounded in the provided source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to: “Use `targetSqsQueueName` for another queue in the same Stacktape config, or `targetSqsQueueArn` for an external queue.” Only say “exactly one” if a validator source is provided.

issues.statement: “Stacktape automatically creates the EventBridge rule and grants the necessary permissions.”
issues.reason: The source confirms that an SQS queue `events` entry currently supports EventBridge delivery into the queue, but it does not describe the generated AWS resources or IAM permission behavior. This is a Stacktape-specific implementation claim and should be grounded in runtime code or omitted.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with source-grounded wording: “Use the queue `events` property to route matching EventBridge events into the queue.”

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the main SQS queue workflow well: basic queues, FIFO, high-throughput FIFO, DLQs, polling, Lambda consumption, connectTo, EventBridge delivery, alarms, referenceable params, and exactly one API reference. I found a few API-completeness and example-demonstrativeness issues that are worth fixing before publication.

issues.statement: The EventBridge integration section only shows `eventBusName` and does not mention the other supported EventBridge source selectors.
issues.reason: Because the draft documents the queue-level `events` property, it should name the supported ways to select the EventBridge bus. The source allows exactly one of `eventBusArn`, `eventBusName`, or `useDefaultBus`; omitting `eventBusArn` and `useDefaultBus` makes the documented path look narrower than the API.
issues.suggestedFix: In the EventBridge integration section, add one sentence before or after the example: “Choose exactly one EventBridge source: `eventBusName` for a Stacktape-managed bus, `eventBusArn` for an existing external bus, or `useDefaultBus` for the default AWS event bus.”

evidence.quote: You must specify only one of `eventBusArn`, `eventBusName`, or `useDefaultBus`.
An SQS queue can receive messages from an EventBridge event bus using the `events` property on the queue.

issues.statement: The page does not mention `policyStatements`, even though custom queue resource policies are the supported way to grant cross-account or service-principal access not covered by `connectTo`.
issues.reason: Most same-stack consumers should use `connectTo`, but queue resource policies are security-relevant when producers are outside the Stacktape stack or when AWS services such as SNS need to send messages. The page has a “Connecting to other resources” section, so readers may reasonably need to know where the boundary of `connectTo` is and what to use beyond it.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short “External producers” or “Queue policies” paragraph: use `connectTo` for Stacktape-managed compute resources, and use `policyStatements` only for cross-account access or specific AWS service principals. Defer details to the API reference rather than enumerating IAM syntax.

evidence.quote: Custom access-control statements added to the queue's resource policy.
Use to grant cross-account access or allow specific AWS services (e.g., SNS) to send messages to this queue.

issues.statement: The EventBridge integration example for a FIFO queue caveat is non-demonstrative because the prose says FIFO queues need `messageGroupId`, but the example uses a standard queue and does not show `messageGroupId`.
issues.reason: The section introduces a specific FIFO-only requirement for `SqsQueueEventBusIntegrationProps.messageGroupId`; the example only demonstrates the standard queue path. A reader configuring EventBridge delivery into a FIFO queue would not see the required shape in the section where the caveat appears.
issues.suggestedFix: Either keep the standard example and add a tiny focused FIFO variant showing `fifoEnabled: true` plus `properties.messageGroupId`, or add `messageGroupId` to the existing example only if the queue is changed to FIFO.

evidence.quote: Message group ID for FIFO queues. Required when the target queue has `fifoEnabled: true`.
For FIFO queues, set `messageGroupId` on the integration to control message ordering.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

issues.statement: When routing EventBridge events into a FIFO queue, set `messageGroupId` on the integration to control message ordering.
issues.reason: The source JSDoc explicitly states messageGroupId is 'Required when the target queue has `fifoEnabled: true`.' A reader who omits it for a FIFO queue will get an AWS error at deploy or runtime since SQS FIFO queues require a MessageGroupId on every message. The word 'required' should appear in the prose.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: 'When routing EventBridge events into a FIFO queue, you must set `messageGroupId` on the integration — SQS requires a message group ID for every message delivered to a FIFO queue. Messages in the same group are processed in strict order; different groups can be processed in parallel.'

evidence.quote: Message group ID for FIFO queues. Required when the target queue has `fifoEnabled: true`.

verifierResults.summary: This is a well-grounded, accurate page. Nearly every Stacktape-specific claim is directly supported by the source types. The three issues found are all low-to-medium severity: (1) the FIFO EventBridge messageGroupId is described as optional when the source says it's required for FIFO queues, (2) the FAQ quotes specific dollar prices that the style guide says to avoid, and (3) the connectTo permissions description is slightly vague about what 'ancillary actions' are granted. No high-severity issues were found — all property names, defaults, alarm types, referenceable parameters, environment variable names, and code examples are correct.

The draft is broadly well grounded in the supplied SQS queue type definitions and related trigger/alarm sources. I found a few Stacktape-specific or source-grounding issues that should be fixed before publication.

issues.statement: "SQS delivers each message to exactly one consumer."
issues.reason: The draft states this as a general SQS behavior, but the same page and the source distinguish standard queues as at-least-once with possible duplicates. The safer sourced claim is that SQS is for one logical consumer workflow, not fan-out, and FIFO is the queue mode with exactly-once delivery language.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: "SQS is designed for one logical consumer workflow per message. Standard queues use at-least-once delivery, so a message can occasionally be delivered more than once; use SNS or EventBridge when every service needs its own copy."

evidence.quote: Creates a FIFO queue that guarantees message order and exactly-once delivery.
FIFO queues have lower throughput (~300 msg/s without batching, ~3,000 with) compared to standard queues.

issues.statement: "A single SQS queue should have only one consumer Lambda function."
issues.reason: The source supports the guidance for one consumer function in the Lambda SQS integration, but the draft expands it into an absolute warning without the source's qualifier and without distinguishing a single logical consumer from scaling/concurrency of one event source mapping. This could mislead readers into thinking SQS cannot be consumed by a scaled Lambda consumer.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the source wording more closely: "Use one Lambda event-source consumer for a queue when each message should be handled once. For fan-out, publish to SNS or EventBridge and give each downstream consumer its own target."

evidence.quote: A single SQS queue should only have one consumer function. For fan-out (multiple consumers for the same message), use an SNS topic or EventBridge event bus instead.

issues.statement: "For Stacktape-managed compute resources ... Stacktape grants the necessary IAM permissions to send messages to, receive messages from, and delete messages in the queue — along with the ancillary actions needed to use the queue URL."
issues.reason: The source explicitly says connectTo grants SQS send/receive/delete and injects ARN, NAME, URL. It does not support the broader phrase "ancillary actions needed to use the queue URL," which implies additional specific IAM actions not shown in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the ancillary-actions phrase and keep the grounded permission summary: "Stacktape grants SQS send, receive, and delete permissions and injects ARN, NAME, and URL variables."

evidence.quote: **`SqsQueue`** — send/receive/delete → `ARN`, `NAME`, `URL`

issues.statement: "If the main queue is FIFO, the DLQ must also be FIFO."

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.

Standard vs FIFO comparison table is well-executed: covers ordering, delivery, throughput, and use cases in a scannable format.

Lambda trigger section correctly notes that the trigger is configured on the Lambda, not the queue, and includes the idempotency caveat.

FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (DLQ, monitoring, container services), AWS service use-case (pricing, max message size, retention), and comparison/choosing (SQS vs SNS, SQS vs Kinesis).

problems: All CodeBlock examples have the `import { ... } from 'stacktape'` line placed BEFORE the `// [!code focus-start]` marker, making the import visible in every rendered snippet. The style guide explicitly lists 'A visible import { ... } from stacktape line in a rendered config example' as a forbidden form and mandatory fix.

The application handler fenced TypeScript block (Lambda handler) uses `console.info` — consistent with the style guide — but also annotates the event type inline as `{ Records: Array<{ body: string; messageId: string }> }`. This is fine technically, but the type is narrower than the actual AWS SQS event shape (missing attributes, awsRegion, etc.), which may mislead users writing production handlers.

The 'Polling and visibility' section example uses `visibilityTimeoutSeconds: 300` without anchoring why 300 — the prose says 'if tasks take 2 minutes, use at least 150 seconds' but then shows 300. Minor inconsistency: the prose guideline implies 150 is sufficient yet the example uses 300 with no explanation for the extra headroom.

The `Tip` callout about DLQ queue-type matching ('if the main queue is FIFO, the DLQ should also be FIFO') is correct AWS guidance, but it is stated as an 'AWS SQS compatibility' requirement — AWS SQS actually enforces this at the API level (rejects the redrive policy if types don't match), so calling it a 'compatibility' suggestion understates the enforcement. This may lead users to treat it as optional.
optionalImprovements: Strengthen the DLQ Tip callout: replace 'For AWS SQS compatibility, use the same queue type for the DLQ as the source queue' with 'AWS SQS requires the DLQ to be the same type as the source queue — a standard queue cannot use a FIFO DLQ and vice versa. Attempting to configure a mismatched type will fail at deploy time.'

In the Lambda handler code example, widen the event type annotation to better reflect production usage (e.g. use `SQSEvent` from `@types/aws-lambda` or note that the simplified inline type omits attributes). This prevents users from building incomplete type contracts in production handlers.

Anchor the `visibilityTimeoutSeconds: 300` value in the 'Polling and visibility' example — either adjust the prose to explain that 300 provides a safe buffer above the 2-minute example task (i.e. 5x headroom), or change the example value to 150 to match the prose's stated threshold.

The pricing paragraph appears twice: once as a standalone paragraph after the title and once in the FAQ. Consider trimming the standalone paragraph to a single sentence and letting the FAQ answer carry the detail — reduces repetition without losing discoverability.

The `maxBatchWindowSeconds: 5` value in the Lambda trigger example is not anchored in prose. Add one sentence explaining that 5 seconds trades a small batching delay for better cost efficiency (fewer invocations at low traffic), and that 0 means invoke immediately on any available message.

The 'Queue policies' section is intentionally brief and links to the API Reference for details — this is correct per the style guide. Consider adding one concrete example of an SNS-to-SQS policy statement to make the section actionable without expanding it significantly.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Self-contained first paragraph (57 words) answers 'what is this and why does it matter' without meta-framing — extractable as a Featured Snippet.

All code examples are complete and copy-pasteable; the Lambda handler and AWS SDK producer examples give readers runnable end-to-end patterns.
Dead-letter queue section explains the 'poison message' problem, provides the 3–5 maxReceiveCount heuristic, and shows when to target an internal vs external DLQ — solid decision support.
'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' are correctly structured as peer H2 sections with concrete alternatives (SNS, Kinesis, direct invocation) rather than vague guidance.
Long polling and visibility timeout recommendations are concrete and opinionated ('Set to 20 for most workloads', 'Set this higher than your expected processing time').
Environment variables table for connectTo is named after the specific example resource (taskQueue → STP_TASK_QUEUE_*), not a generic placeholder.

problems: FAQ is skewed ~7:2:1 toward Stacktape-specific questions, with only two genuine AWS use-case questions (pricing, standard vs FIFO) and one comparison question (vs Kinesis). Missing high-intent searches like 'What is SQS message delivery latency?', 'How does SQS compare to Kafka or RabbitMQ?', 'What happens to messages when a consumer service goes offline?', or 'SQS vs Redis as a task queue?' Penalizes completeness.

`visibilityTimeoutSeconds: 300` in the polling/visibility code example is not explicitly connected to the prose heuristic ('if tasks take 2 minutes, use at least 150 seconds'). Readers may treat 300 as a required or magic default. Minor property-value anchor gap.

The `delayMessagesSecond` configurable property (delay queues, 0–900 seconds) appears only in FAQ Q8 and the basic example defaults sentence — it is absent from the main content body. Users hunting for the delay queue feature will not discover it from any section heading.

optionalImprovements: Rebalance FAQ to achieve a closer 4:3:3 split across (1) Stacktape-specific, (2) AWS use-case, and (3) comparison questions. Replace two or three Stacktape-specific questions with: 'What is the typical SQS message delivery latency?', 'How does SQS compare to Apache Kafka for event streaming?', 'What happens to messages when my consumer is offline for hours?', or 'When should I use SQS vs a Redis-backed task queue?'

In the 'Polling and visibility' section, add one sentence immediately before or after the visibilityTimeoutSeconds code example that connects the value 300 to the stated scenario: e.g., 'In this example, tasks may take up to 2 minutes, so 300 seconds leaves a comfortable buffer before the message reappears.'

Remove the near-verbatim pricing repetition between the intro paragraph and FAQ Q2. Either keep pricing only in the intro callout and summarize Q2 differently, or fold the intro pricing into the FAQ and remove the standalone paragraph above 'When to use'.
